explicit_text: My grandparents lived in Syria, where both my parents were born. My father grew up attending the Presbyterian Mission School in Antioch, Syria, during a time when the area was partly under Turkish control. Syria was the starting point of my family's journey before they immigrated to the United States in the early 1900s.

This country was the starting point of my family's journey before they immigrated to the United States in the early 1900s.

explicit_text: My mother's father was the keeper of the vineyards in Tripoli, but not the Tripoli in Libya; in Arabic, it's called Troblos. This part of my family came from that region, though I was not told much more about them. It was a distinct branch from my father's family, who were from Antioch.

explicit_text: My father grew up in Antioch, Syria, where he attended the Presbyterian Mission School. This was a significant place for him because he taught at the girls' school there before coming to America around 1907 or 1908. Antioch was under Turkish rule at the time, which influenced my father's decision to emigrate to avoid conscription into the Turkish Army.

explicit_text: My mother and her brother sold fancy linens door-to-door in the Great Lakes area before settling in Tulsa. This region had many resort spots where wealthier people bought imported goods from peddlers like them. It was part of their early American experience before moving west.

explicit_text: My mother and her brother lived in Butte, Montana, a mining town, for a few years before moving to Tulsa. They initially set up a shop there, but my uncle decided to move south after noting the gold rush was not the right fit. Mother was left to manage the shop alone at age 18 before relocating to Tulsa around 1912.

explicit_text: Tulsa became the permanent home for my family starting in 1912 when my mother and uncle settled here during the oil boom. They established a store selling oriental rugs and linens, which served the growing wealth of the city. My father joined them in 1924, and the family business operated in Tulsa downtown for over four decades.

explicit_text: E & A Dumit was the original name of the store my mother and her brother Elias set up when they arrived in Tulsa. The name came from their initials, Elias and Anisa Dumit. This store was the foundation of our family business before my father joined and the name changed.

explicit_text: When my father joined the family business in 1924, the store was renamed Dumit's Rugs and Linens. We sold fancy linens from places like Madeira, Portugal, China, and also oriental rugs, lamps, and other exotic items. The store moved several times downtown Tulsa and operated until 1956, when it closed after my uncle's tragic accident.

explicit_text: Our first store location in Tulsa was at 4th and Main, right next to the Majestic Theatre. My mother recalled a hole in the bathroom wall through which she could watch the movies playing at the theatre during quiet times. This proximity to the theatre was part of our early Tulsa experience.

explicit_text: I was hired by The University of Tulsa in September 1955, just before our family store closed. At TU, I split my time between running the student-operated radio station KWGS and teaching broadcasting courses. This new role helped me transition away from the family business after my uncle's death.

explicit_text: When my mother and uncle arrived in Tulsa in 1912, they immediately joined the First Presbyterian Church. For many years, I sat in the front pew there with them, and people often confused my uncle for my father because my father was more private in his devotions. The church was central to our family's community life in Tulsa.
